content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 7, 1990


Neil Papiano
Iverson, Yoakum, Papiano and Hatch
One Wilshire Building
624 South Grand Avenue, 27th Floor
Los Angeles, CA  90017-3328

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-90-399

Dear Mr. Papiano:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing application of the campaign contribution provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1  You do not seek our advice
regarding a particular decision before you but rather seek general
guidance. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation
18329(c) (copy enclosed).2  This advice is prospective only; we do
not advise regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

                              QUESTIONS

     1.  From time to time you solicit contributions from your
clients and receive checks from them evidencing their contribu-
tion. If you transmit these checks to a candidate or to the
candidate's supporters either at a dinner or by mail, are you an
"intermediary" for purposes of the Act?

     2.  From time to your clients solicit contributions from
you and you make personal contributions evidenced by checks on
which your name appears and which are reported in your name.
Under the provisions of the Act, is the person who solicits the
contribution an intermediary?


_________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-399
Page 2

     3.  If you give a contribution in the form of a check bearing
your name to someone to deliver to a candidate or to a candidate's
representative and then you attend the political function or dinner for which the contribution was requested, is the person
receiving your check an intermediary?  Would the result be different if a friend or someone in your law firm used the tickets for
which you have paid?

     4.  What are the reporting obligations of an intermediary?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  If you transmit a contribution from another person to a
candidate or committee either at a dinner or by mail you are an
"intermediary" for purposes of the Act.

     2 and 3.  A person who solicits a contribution from you is
not an intermediary.  However, if you give a contribution in the
form of a check bearing your name to someone to deliver to a
candidate or to a candidate's representative, the person who
delivers your contribution is an intermediary.  Attendance at a
fundraiser is of no relevance for purposes of the reporting
statutes.  Thus, whether you attend the function yourself or make
a gift of your ticket to someone else to attend the event, the
result does not change.

     4.  An intermediary must disclose to the recipient certain
information pertaining to the contributor and similar information
pertaining to the intermediary.

FACTS

     From time to time your clients ask you to make contributions
to candidates for elective office or to support a proposition or
other ballot measures.  On most of those occasions, because of
client relationships, you make a contribution.  In addition, from
time to time you request your clients to make contributions in
response to requests made to you by candidates or their supporters
or even by other clients.  In all of these instances, the actual
source of the contribution is clearly indicated by the name
printed on the check.  In each instance the contribution is appropriately reported by the donor.  You assume the donee similarly
reports receipt of the contribution.

     You seek our advice to determine under what circumstances you
are acting as an "intermediary" for purposes of your reporting
obligations under the Act.  It is your belief that when the name
of the contributor appears on the check and both the contributor
and the recipient report the contribution there are no
"intermediaries" involved and no further reporting is required
under the Act.

File No. I-90-399
Page 3

ANALYSIS

As amended by Proposition 73, the Act places limits on
contributions. (Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303.) The Act also
prohibits anonymous contributions of $100 or more in a fiscal year
(Section 84304) and requires contributors to disclose their legal
names when making contributions (Section 84301). Additionally,
the Act requires candidates and their committees as well as ballot
measure committees to disclose in their campaign statements the
source of all contributions of $100 or more. (Section 84211.)

The Act imposes certain disclosure requirements when
contributions are made in the name of another person or through an
intermediary. Section 84302 provides that:

No person shall make a contribution on behalf
of another, or while acting as the intermediary or
agent of another, without disclosing to the
recipient of the contribution both his own full
name and street address, occupation, and the name
of his employer, if any, or his principal place of
business if he is self-employed, and the full name
and street address, occupation, and the name of
employer, if any, or principal place of business if
self-employed, of the other person. The recipient
of the contribution shall include in his campaign
statement the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or the
principal place of business if self-employed, of
both the intermediary and the contributor.

Section 84302.

Section 84302.5 provides a definition of "intermediary" as
follows:

A person is an intermediary for transmittal of
a contribution if he or she delivers to a candidate
or committee a contribution from another person
unless such contribution is from the person's
employer, immediate family or an association to
which the person belongs. No person who is the
treasurer of the committee to which the contribution is made or is the candidate who controls the
committee to which the contribution is made shall
be an intermediary for such a contribution.

Section 84302.5.

For purposes of further clarification of the statutory provisions, the Commission has adopted Regulation 18432.5 (copy
enclosed) which provides that a person is an intermediary if the
recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the

File No. I-90-399
Page 4

contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true
source of the contribution. Also, a person is an intermediary for
transmittal of a contribution if he or she meets both of the following requirements:

(1) The person delivers, mails, or otherwise
transmits a contribution to a candidate or committee, either personally or through an agent.

(2) The person is not the true source of the
contribution.

Regulation 18432.5(b).

Accordingly, with the exception of committee treasurers and
persons who deliver contributions from an employer, immediate family or association to which the person belongs, all other persons
who deliver contributions from another person to a candidate or
committee are intermediaries, even when the name of the
contributor is printed on the check evidencing the contribution.

The manner of delivery of the contribution is of no
consequence under the Act. Whenever the contribution is
delivered, mailed, or otherwise transmitted to the recipient
either personally or through an agent by a person other than the
contributor, the person delivering the contribution is an
intermediary. Thus, when you make a contribution evidenced by a
personal check which displays your name and you give your check to
another person for delivery to the recipient candidate or committee, the person delivering your check is an intermediary. If your
contribution consists of purchase of tickets to a fundraiser and
you do not attend the event, anyone may make use of the tickets.
Persons attending the event who do not make contributions and who
do not act as intermediaries for the delivery of contributions
incur no reporting obligations under the Act.

Both the intermediary and the recipient of the contribution
are subject to the disclosure requirements of the Act. Whenever
you deliver a contribution from one of your clients to a candidate
or his or her representative, you are an intermediary. As an
intermediary, you must disclose to the recipient of the contribution your full name and street address, occupation, and the name
of your employer, if any, or your principal place of business if
you are self-employed. You must also disclose the full name and
street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any,
or principal place of business if self-employed, of the
contributor. The fact that the name of the contributor is printed
on the check evidencing the contribution does not relieve the
intermediary from the duty to disclose information regarding the
contributor.

The recipient of the contribution also has disclosure obligation. A candidate or committee which receives a contribution must

File No. I-90-399
Page 5

include in its campaign statement the name of the intermediary and
other information disclosed pursuant to Section 84302, supra. If
the recipient knows or has reason to know that a contribution is
made by an intermediary, the recipient candidate or committee must
disclose the information provided by the intermediary. (Regula-
tion 18432.5(d) and (e).)

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]
By: Blanca M. Breeze
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:plh

Enclosures

sections.analysis:
As amended by Proposition 73, the Act places limits on contributions. (Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303.) The Act also prohibits anonymous contributions of $100 or more in a fiscal year (Section 84304) and requires contributors to disclose their legal names when making contributions (Section 84301). Additionally, the Act requires candidates and their committees as well as ballot measure committees to disclose in their campaign statements the source of all contributions of $100 or more. (Section 84211.)

The Act imposes certain disclosure requirements when contributions are made in the name of another person or through an intermediary. Section 84302 provides that:

No person shall make a contribution on behalf of another, or while acting as the intermediary or agent of another, without disclosing to the recipient of the contribution both his own full name and street address, occupation, and the name of his employer, if any, or his principal place of business if he is self-employed, and the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of employer, if any, or principal place of business if self-employed, of the other person. The recipient of the contribution shall include in his campaign statement the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or the principal place of business if self-employed, of both the intermediary and the contributor.

Section 84302.

Section 84302.5 provides a definition of "intermediary" as follows:

A person is an intermediary for transmittal of a contribution if he or she delivers to a candidate or committee a contribution from another person unless such contribution is from the person's employer, immediate family or an association to which the person belongs. No person who is the treasurer of the committee to which the contribution is made or is the candidate who controls the committee to which the contribution is made shall be an intermediary for such a contribution.

Section 84302.5.

For purposes of further clarification of the statutory provisions, the Commission has adopted Regulation 18432.5 (copy enclosed) which provides that a person is an intermediary if the recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the

contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true source of the contribution. Also, a person is an intermediary for transmittal of a contribution if he or she meets both of the following requirements:

(1) The person delivers, mails, or otherwise transmits a contribution to a candidate or committee, either personally or through an agent.

(2) The person is not the true source of the contribution.

Regulation 18432.5(b).

Accordingly, with the exception of committee treasurers and persons who deliver contributions from an employer, immediate family or association to which the person belongs, all other persons who deliver contributions from another person to a candidate or committee are intermediaries, even when the name of the contributor is printed on the check evidencing the contribution.

The manner of delivery of the contribution is of no consequence under the Act. Whenever the contribution is delivered, mailed, or otherwise transmitted to the recipient either personally or through an agent by a person other than the contributor, the person delivering the contribution is an intermediary. Thus, when you make a contribution evidenced by a personal check which displays your name and you give your check to another person for delivery to the recipient candidate or committee, the person delivering your check is an intermediary. If your contribution consists of purchase of tickets to a fundraiser and you do not attend the event, anyone may make use of the tickets. Persons attending the event who do not make contributions and who do not act as intermediaries for the delivery of contributions incur no reporting obligations under the Act.

Both the intermediary and the recipient of the contribution are subject to the disclosure requirements of the Act. Whenever you deliver a contribution from one of your clients to a candidate or his or her representative, you are an intermediary. As an intermediary, you must disclose to the recipient of the contribution your full name and street address, occupation, and the name of your employer, if any, or your principal place of business if you are self-employed. You must also disclose the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or principal place of business if self-employed, of the contributor. The fact that the name of the contributor is printed on the check evidencing the contribution does not relieve the intermediary from the duty to disclose information regarding the contributor.

The recipient of the contribution also has disclosure obligation. A candidate or committee which receives a contribution must

include in its campaign statement the name of the intermediary and other information disclosed pursuant to Section 84302, supra. If the recipient knows or has reason to know that a contribution is made by an intermediary, the recipient candidate or committee must disclose the information provided by the intermediary. (Regulation 18432.5(d) and (e).)

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.
